Learning ThinkPHP Day 2: SQL Basics — Working With Databases

Welcome to the first proper post of the ThinkPHP series, brave reader. Posts 1 through 4 are all about the basics of SQL commands and operations. Once we've got those down, we'll start moving into ThinkPHP for real. So let's get going!
mentioned we'd be using XAMPP, that super

all-in-one quick-setup toolkit. A lot of people have already covered how to install XAMPP and how it works, so I won't go over the install process here. Without further ado, let's jump straight into the built-in phpMyAdmin.
You can get in by clicking the Admin button in XAMPP
Once you're inside phpMyAdmin, click the SQL tab up top to switch to the SQL page. From there we'll start getting to know the basic database commands.

Quick aside on what phpMyAdmin actually is. Normally we work with MySQL through the command line (CMD), but phpMyAdmin is a web GUI built on top of PHP. The main point is that it lets you run all kinds of SQL commands and actions directly through the interface, and honestly it's pretty powerful. I won't dig into the interface much here though — we're just here to learn SQL.
Pretty much every website has a dedicated place to store its data, and that place is the
. You can think of a database like a bank vault. So the first thing we'll learn is how to create one. Say I want to create a database called
You'll also want to run the command below. This one sets the character set and collation for the database. Otherwise the default is usually
, and if you skip this step you'll end up with garbled data going in! So please remember to set the character set and collation:
ALTER DATABASE it_think_sql CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_general_ci;
ALTER DATABASE [database name] CHARACTER SET [character set] COLLATE [collation];

Here's another nice thing about phpMyAdmin: we can paste both SQL commands above and run them in one go, instead of running one, then running the next. If you were doing this through CMD, you'd have to run them line by line.
Once it's done, you'll see the database we just added show up in the database list on the left.

Sometimes you'll want to see which databases currently exist. To put it in our scenario: say I'm the bank's head office with a ton of branches, and I want to know which branch vaults I've got right now. We can use the following command to find out:

One last thing I want to flag:

is often a really dangerous operation. If you've been following along, you'll have noticed that when you run a delete SQL command, you don't get any kind of confirmation prompt (some GUIs will at least ask "are you sure you want to delete this database?"). It just deletes the database you pointed at, immediately. So before deleting a database, please make sure you've got a backup and double-check everything, so you don't accidentally wipe out data you really care about!

Set database character set and collation

Welcome to the first real post of the ThinkPHP series. Posts 1 through 4 cover the basics of SQL commands and operations, and once we're comfortable with those we'll jump into ThinkPHP proper. This time we start at the very bottom: creating, listing, and deleting databases in MySQL through phpMyAdmin. Let's get going!
